written: The theory of innate language ability, first proposed by Noam Chomsky, remains one of the most influential frameworks in modern linguistics.

spoken: So universal grammar is the idea that all human languages share some deep underlying structure — pretty mind-blowing when you think about it.
written: The concept of universal grammar suggests that the capacity for language is hardwired into the human brain from birth.

spoken: Sign language is a visual form of language — it uses hand shapes and movements instead of sounds to convey meaning.
written: A visual form of language, such as sign language, demonstrates that linguistic communication is not exclusively dependent on auditory channels.

spoken: I've always wanted to see the Northern Lights, so a trip to the Arctic Circle is definitely on my bucket list.
written: The Arctic Circle is experiencing some of the most rapid environmental changes on the planet due to rising global temperatures.

spoken: British weather is ridiculously changeable — you can literally experience all four seasons in a single afternoon.

spoken: We had a cracking good time at the festival last weekend — the music was absolutely fantastic.

spoken: There's been a lot of fluctuation in petrol prices lately — it's impossible to predict what you'll pay next week.
written: Significant fluctuations in global temperatures have been linked to changes in ocean current patterns and atmospheric composition.

spoken: A massive chunk of ice shelf broke off in Antarctica last year — it was literally the size of a small country.
written: The disintegration of the Larsen B ice shelf in 2002 served as a stark warning about the accelerating effects of climate change.

spoken: The closer you get to the equator, the lower the latitude, and generally the hotter it gets.
written: Climate conditions vary significantly with latitude, influencing the distribution of ecosystems and human settlement patterns.

spoken: The monsoon season in India is no joke — it rains so heavily that whole streets get flooded within minutes.
written: The monsoon system plays a critical role in the agricultural economies of South and Southeast Asia, determining crop yields and food security.

spoken: Did you know that seasons in the Northern Hemisphere are the complete opposite of those in the Southern Hemisphere?
written: The Northern Hemisphere contains approximately 90% of the world's population and is characterised by greater landmass and climatic diversity.

spoken: Emperor penguins are amazing — they survive in temperatures of minus fifty degrees and somehow don't freeze to death.
written: The emperor penguin has evolved a range of physiological adaptations that enable it to survive in the extreme conditions of Antarctica.

spoken: The polar regions are warming up way faster than the rest of the planet, which is honestly pretty alarming.
written: The polar regions serve as critical indicators of global climate change, with rising temperatures causing unprecedented ice loss.

spoken: There's something magical about twilight — that moment when the sky turns orange and everything goes quiet.
written: Many nocturnal species begin their activity during the twilight hours, taking advantage of reduced visibility to avoid predators.

spoken: There's a lot of variability in exam results across different schools, which suggests the quality of teaching really matters.
written: The variability of climate conditions across different regions presents a significant challenge for developing universal environmental policies.

spoken: Living near an active volcano sounds terrifying, but loads of people actually do it because the soil is super fertile.
written: Communities situated in the vicinity of an active volcano face ongoing risks that require comprehensive emergency preparedness strategies.

spoken: The earthquake was devastating — thankfully the casualties were lower than expected because of the early warning system.
written: The number of casualties resulting from natural disasters can be significantly reduced through the implementation of effective early warning systems.

spoken: The traffic after the concert was absolutely chaotic — it took us two hours to drive what normally takes fifteen minutes.
written: The chaotic aftermath of a natural disaster often overwhelms local emergency services, necessitating international humanitarian assistance.

spoken: Coastal subsidence is a real problem for cities like Jakarta — the ground is literally sinking because of all the groundwater being pumped out.
written: Coastal subsidence, exacerbated by excessive groundwater extraction, poses an existential threat to low-lying urban areas worldwide.

spoken: Scientists are trying to concentrate monitoring efforts on the areas most at risk from volcanic eruptions.
written: It is essential to concentrate monitoring efforts along active fault lines to improve the accuracy of earthquake prediction systems.

spoken: Crustal movement is what causes earthquakes — basically, the Earth's plates are constantly shifting and bumping into each other.
written: Crustal movement along tectonic boundaries is the primary driver of seismic activity and geological transformation across the Earth's surface.

spoken: The crustal plates beneath our feet are moving all the time, even though we can't feel it — it's kind of wild to think about.
written: Crustal deformation patterns provide valuable data for understanding the dynamics of tectonic plate interactions.

spoken: The bridge had to be closed because engineers detected some deformation in the support structure — safety first, I guess.
written: Structural deformation caused by seismic activity can compromise the integrity of buildings, necessitating rigorous engineering standards.

spoken: Some microplastics in the ocean are so tiny they're barely detectable with the naked eye.
written: Even trace amounts of pollutants are now detectable using advanced spectroscopic techniques, enabling earlier environmental interventions.

written: Statistical distortion in research findings can arise from sampling bias, leading to inaccurate conclusions and misguided policy recommendations.

written: The relationship between humans and domestic animals has evolved over millennia, shaping both agricultural practices and emotional well-being.

spoken: The earthquake intensity was measured at 7.8 on the Richter scale — that's strong enough to bring down entire buildings.
written: Earthquake intensity is assessed using various scales, including the Modified Mercalli Index, which evaluates the severity of damage and human impact.

spoken: Scientists are researching every possible earthquake precursor — like changes in groundwater levels or unusual animal behaviour.
written: Identifying a reliable earthquake precursor remains one of the most elusive goals in the field of seismology.

earthquake prediction.spoken: I reckon earthquake prediction's getting way better these days with all the new tech they've got.
earthquake prediction.written: Earthquake prediction remains one of the most challenging scientific endeavours, as no reliable method has yet been developed to forecast the precise timing of seismic events.

the richter scale.spoken: So basically, the Richter scale measures how strong an earthquake is, right? Like, a 7 is massive.
the richter scale.written: The Richter scale, which quantifies the energy released during an earthquake, has largely been superseded by the moment magnitude scale in modern seismology.

earthquake-triggered mudflow.spoken: After the quake, there was this huge earthquake-triggered mudflow that just wiped out a whole village.
earthquake-triggered mudflow.written: Earthquake-triggered mudflows pose a significant secondary hazard, often causing devastation far beyond the epicentral zone itself.

electrical resistivity.spoken: They use electrical resistivity surveys to figure out what's going on beneath the surface before drilling.
electrical resistivity.written: Electrical resistivity measurements are frequently employed in geophysical surveys to detect subsurface anomalies indicative of tectonic stress accumulation.

electromagnetic field.spoken: Apparently, changes in the electromagnetic field can sometimes signal that an earthquake's coming.
electromagnetic field.written: Variations in the electromagnetic field have been observed prior to seismic events, although the underlying mechanisms are not yet fully understood.

epicentral zone.spoken: The epicentral zone is basically where all the worst damage happens, closest to where the quake hits.
epicentral zone.written: Structures situated within the epicentral zone are subjected to the most intense ground motion, necessitating stringent building codes.

fault slippage.spoken: Fault slippage is what actually causes the ground to shake during an earthquake, isn't it?
fault slippage.written: Fault slippage occurs when accumulated tectonic stress exceeds the frictional resistance along a geological fault line.

fault zone.spoken: You wouldn't want to build anything important right on top of a fault zone, obviously.
fault zone.written: Urban development within an active fault zone should be carefully regulated, as the likelihood of seismic activity is substantially elevated.

fault.spoken: There's a major fault running right through the city, which is a bit worrying to be honest.
fault.written: A geological fault is a fracture in the Earth's crust along which displacement has occurred, and it serves as the primary locus for earthquake generation.

federal support.spoken: The community's been asking for federal support to help rebuild after the disaster.
federal support.written: Federal support for disaster preparedness programmes has been shown to significantly reduce casualties in earthquake-prone regions.

foreshock.spoken: A foreshock is like a smaller earthquake that happens before the main one, kind of a warning sign.
foreshock.written: Foreshocks, which precede the principal seismic event, are typically identified only in retrospect, limiting their usefulness as early warning indicators.

fracture zone dynamics.spoken: Understanding fracture zone dynamics is key to predicting how earthquakes might behave in the future.
fracture zone dynamics.written: Research into fracture zone dynamics has revealed that stress redistribution following one event can trigger subsequent ruptures along adjacent segments.

gather data.spoken: We need to gather data from as many monitoring stations as possible to get a clear picture.
gather data.written: It is essential to gather data from diverse sources in order to build comprehensive models of seismic risk assessment.

gauge.spoken: They've set up this new gauge to measure the intensity of ground shaking in real time.
gauge.written: Precision instruments are used to gauge the magnitude and frequency of seismic waves, providing critical information for hazard evaluation.

geophysical observatory.spoken: The Geophysical Observatory has been monitoring tremors in the region for decades now.
geophysical observatory.written: The Geophysical Observatory plays a vital role in the continuous monitoring of tectonic activity across seismically active zones.

geophysicist.spoken: My friend's a geophysicist, and she says the Pacific Ring of Fire is one of the most dangerous places on Earth.
geophysicist.written: A geophysicist specialises in the physical properties and processes of the Earth, including the study of earthquakes and volcanic phenomena.

ground crack.spoken: After the earthquake, there were ground cracks everywhere — some were really wide.
ground crack.written: Ground cracks resulting from intense seismic shaking can compromise the structural integrity of foundations and underground utilities.

ground fracturing.spoken: Ground fracturing from the earthquake made the roads completely impassable.
ground fracturing.written: Ground fracturing is a common consequence of strong seismic events and can severely impede emergency response operations.

ground motion.spoken: The ground motion during that earthquake was so intense that people couldn't even stand up.
ground motion.written: The characterisation of ground motion is fundamental to the design of earthquake-resistant structures in vulnerable regions.

ground settling.spoken: There's been some ground settling near the coast since the last earthquake, which is a bit concerning.
ground settling.written: Ground settling following seismic activity may indicate subsurface voids or liquefaction, both of which require immediate geotechnical investigation.

hydraulic system.spoken: The dam's hydraulic system was damaged in the quake, so they had to shut it down for repairs.
hydraulic system.written: The hydraulic system of critical infrastructure must be designed to withstand seismic forces to prevent catastrophic failure during an earthquake.

immobilise.spoken: The earthquake basically immobilised the entire transport network for days.
immobilise.written: A major seismic event can immobilise emergency services, thereby exacerbating the humanitarian crisis in affected areas.

inconclusive research.spoken: There's been a lot of inconclusive research on earthquake prediction, to be honest — nobody's cracked it yet.
inconclusive research.written: Inconclusive research into seismic precursors highlights the inherent complexity of earthquake mechanics and the limitations of current methodologies.

increased strain.spoken: Scientists have detected increased strain along the fault, which could mean a big quake is on the way.
increased strain.written: Increased strain along tectonic plate boundaries is routinely monitored to assess the probability of future seismic events.

ionised groundwater.spoken: Some researchers think changes in ionised groundwater could be linked to impending earthquakes.
ionised groundwater.written: The chemical composition of ionised groundwater has been proposed as a potential indicator of subsurface stress changes preceding an earthquake.

landslide.spoken: The earthquake triggered a massive landslide that blocked the main road into the valley.
landslide.written: Landslides triggered by seismic activity represent a significant secondary hazard, often causing casualties and infrastructure damage independent of the earthquake itself.

lava bedrock.spoken: The whole area is built on lava bedrock, which actually helps absorb some of the seismic energy.
lava bedrock.written: The presence of lava bedrock beneath a settlement can significantly influence the propagation characteristics of seismic waves.

mauna loa.spoken: Mauna Loa in Hawaii is one of the biggest active volcanoes in the world, apparently.
mauna loa.written: Mauna Loa, located in Hawaii, is among the largest active volcanoes on Earth and is continuously monitored for signs of eruption.

microquake.spoken: You can't even feel a microquake, but the instruments pick them up all the time.
microquake.written: Microquakes, though imperceptible to humans, provide valuable data for understanding stress accumulation along fault systems.

moment magnitude scale.spoken: The moment magnitude scale is actually more accurate than the Richter one for really big earthquakes.
moment magnitude scale.written: The moment magnitude scale has become the preferred measure for quantifying earthquake size, as it accounts for the total energy released across the entire fault surface.

monitoring equipment.spoken: They've installed a load of new monitoring equipment around the volcano just in case it erupts.
monitoring equipment.written: The deployment of advanced monitoring equipment in seismically active regions is essential for the timely issuance of public warnings.

non-damaging earthquake.spoken: That was just a non-damaging earthquake — nothing to worry about, really.
non-damaging earthquake.written: Even non-damaging earthquakes should be recorded and analysed, as they contribute to a more complete understanding of regional seismicity.

plate tectonics.spoken: Plate tectonics basically explains why earthquakes and volcanoes happen where they do.
plate tectonics.written: The theory of plate tectonics provides a unifying framework for understanding the distribution of earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain ranges across the globe.

pre-earthquake behaviour.spoken: Animals sometimes show weird pre-earthquake behaviour, like acting all restless before a big quake.
pre-earthquake behaviour.written: The study of pre-earthquake behaviour in animal species remains a controversial yet intriguing area of seismological research.

radioactive gas.spoken: There's concern that seismic activity might release trapped radioactive gas from underground.
radioactive gas.written: The release of radioactive gas from subsurface reservoirs during seismic events poses a potential environmental and health hazard.

richter magnitude scale.spoken: The Richter magnitude scale was developed back in the 1930s, but it's still what most people know.
richter magnitude scale.written: Although the Richter magnitude scale was a pioneering contribution to seismology, it has been largely replaced by more comprehensive measurement systems.

rocks' fabric.spoken: The rocks' fabric in an area tells you a lot about the kind of stresses it's been under.
rocks' fabric.written: The analysis of rocks' fabric provides critical insights into the deformation history and mechanical properties of a geological formation.

rupture zone.spoken: The rupture zone from that earthquake stretched for over 200 kilometres along the coast.
rupture zone.written: The extent of the rupture zone is a key factor in determining the overall magnitude and impact of a seismic event.

rupture.spoken: When a fault ruptures, all that stored energy gets released in seconds — it's terrifying.
rupture.written: The rupture of a geological fault initiates the propagation of seismic waves, which are responsible for the destructive ground shaking experienced during an earthquake.

seismic.spoken: Japan's got some of the best seismic building codes in the world, haven't they?
seismic.written: Seismic resilience in urban infrastructure can be significantly enhanced through the adoption of evidence-based engineering standards.

seismic hazard zones.spoken: If you're living in one of the seismic hazard zones, you really need to have an emergency plan.
seismic hazard zones.written: The delineation of seismic hazard zones enables governments to implement targeted mitigation strategies and allocate resources more effectively.

seismic wave.spoken: Seismic waves travel through the Earth at different speeds depending on the material they pass through.
seismic wave.written: The analysis of seismic wave propagation patterns is instrumental in determining the internal structure of the Earth.

seismically active area.spoken: Building a nuclear plant in a seismically active area seems like a pretty risky idea to me.
seismically active area.written: Constructing critical infrastructure in a seismically active area demands rigorous risk assessment and adherence to the highest engineering standards.

seismogram.spoken: The seismogram from yesterday's quake showed some really unusual patterns, apparently.
seismogram.written: The seismogram recorded at the station revealed a complex waveform consistent with a multi-fault rupture event.

seismograph.spoken: A seismograph is basically the instrument that detects and records earthquakes.
seismograph.written: The invention of the seismograph in the late nineteenth century revolutionised the scientific study of earthquakes.

seismologist.spoken: A seismologist on the news said we should expect aftershocks for the next few weeks.
seismologist.written: A seismologist's expertise is indispensable in interpreting seismic data and advising policymakers on earthquake preparedness.

seismology.spoken: Seismology's come a long way, but predicting earthquakes is still pretty much impossible.
seismology.written: Seismology has made remarkable advances in understanding earthquake mechanics, yet the reliable prediction of seismic events remains an elusive goal.

seismometer.spoken: They've put a seismometer right near the fault line to monitor any tiny movements.
seismometer.written: High-sensitivity seismometers are deployed across tectonically active regions to detect even the slightest ground vibrations.

sensor.spoken: The new sensor they've installed can detect tremors so small you'd never feel them yourself.
sensor.written: Advanced sensor technology has greatly improved the accuracy and speed of earthquake early warning systems worldwide.

structural weakness.spoken: The building had a structural weakness that nobody noticed until the earthquake hit.
structural weakness.written: Identifying structural weakness in existing buildings is a critical step in reducing earthquake vulnerability in urban areas.

submarine communication.spoken: Earthquakes under the sea can actually damage submarine communication cables, which is a real problem.
submarine communication.written: Submarine communication infrastructure is vulnerable to seismic disturbances, particularly in regions where cables cross active fault lines.

the united states geological survey.spoken: The United States Geological Survey is the go-to source for earthquake info in America.
the united states geological survey.written: The United States Geological Survey provides authoritative data on seismic events and plays a central role in national hazard assessment.

usgs.spoken: I always check the USGS website whenever there's an earthquake to see how strong it was.
usgs.written: The USGS is widely regarded as one of the most reliable institutions for the monitoring and analysis of seismic activity globally.

tsunami.spoken: The 2011 tsunami in Japan was absolutely devastating — it was triggered by a massive offshore earthquake.
tsunami.written: A tsunami generated by an undersea earthquake can travel across entire ocean basins at speeds exceeding 700 kilometres per hour.

volcanic activity.spoken: There's been a lot of volcanic activity in the region lately, so people are getting a bit nervous.
volcanic activity.written: Volcanic activity is often associated with tectonic plate boundaries, where the movement of magma can trigger both eruptions and seismic events.

atmospheric moisture.spoken: Atmospheric moisture plays a huge role in weather patterns — like, it basically drives the whole system.
atmospheric moisture.written: Atmospheric moisture content has been shown to influence the intensity and frequency of extreme weather events across the globe.

celsius.spoken: The average global temperature has risen by about one point one degrees Celsius since pre-industrial times.
celsius.written: A rise of two degrees Celsius in global mean temperature is widely regarded as the critical threshold beyond which climate impacts become catastrophic.

climatologist.spoken: A climatologist I heard on a podcast said we're not doing nearly enough to tackle climate change.
climatologist.written: Climatologists have consistently warned that current emission trajectories are incompatible with the goals set out in the Paris Agreement.

computer simulation.spoken: They ran a computer simulation to see what would happen if global temperatures kept rising.
computer simulation.written: Computer simulation of climate systems enables researchers to model complex interactions between the atmosphere, oceans, and terrestrial ecosystems.

condense.spoken: When water vapour rises and cools, it condenses into tiny droplets and forms clouds.
condense.written: As moist air ascends and cools, water vapour begins to condense, releasing latent heat that further drives atmospheric circulation.

drift.spoken: The ocean currents cause warm water to drift from the tropics towards the poles, affecting the climate.
drift.written: The drift of ocean currents plays a fundamental role in the redistribution of thermal energy across the planet.

ecuador.spoken: Ecuador's got a really diverse climate because of the Andes and the Amazon basin.
ecuador.written: Ecuador's geographical diversity, ranging from coastal lowlands to Andean highlands, presents unique challenges for climate adaptation strategies.

evaporated.spoken: The lake basically evaporated during the heatwave — there was almost nothing left.
evaporated.written: Increased surface temperatures have led to higher rates of water being evaporated from reservoirs, exacerbating water scarcity in arid regions.

geophysical.spoken: Geophysical surveys can reveal a lot about how the climate's changed over thousands of years.
geophysical.written: Geophysical methods, including the analysis of ice cores and sediment layers, provide invaluable evidence of historical climate variability.

greenhouse gas.spoken: We really need to cut greenhouse gas emissions if we want to slow down global warming.
greenhouse gas.written: The accumulation of greenhouse gas in the atmosphere, primarily from fossil fuel combustion, is the principal driver of contemporary climate change.

hydrologic cycle.spoken: The hydrologic cycle is basically how water moves around — evaporation, rain, rivers, and back again.
hydrologic cycle.written: The hydrologic cycle, comprising evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and runoff, is fundamentally altered by rising global temperatures.

ice core.spoken: By drilling ice cores in Antarctica, scientists can see what the atmosphere was like hundreds of thousands of years ago.
ice core.written: Ice cores extracted from polar ice sheets contain trapped air bubbles that serve as a proxy for past atmospheric composition and temperature.

ice storm.spoken: That ice storm last winter was brutal — it knocked out power for nearly a week.
ice storm.written: Ice storms, which are projected to increase in frequency under certain climate scenarios, pose a severe threat to infrastructure and public safety.

jet stream.spoken: The jet stream's been all over the place lately, which is why we keep getting these extreme weather events.
jet stream.written: Changes in the jet stream pattern have been linked to the amplification of weather extremes, including prolonged heatwaves and cold spells.

kelvin wave.spoken: Kelvin waves in the ocean are a big part of how El Nino gets started in the Pacific.
kelvin wave.written: Kelvin waves, which propagate along the equatorial thermocline, play a critical role in the onset and development of the El Nino Southern Oscillation.

killer tornado.spoken: A killer tornado can destroy an entire neighbourhood in just a matter of minutes.
killer tornado.written: The frequency of killer tornado events has prompted significant investment in early warning systems and community preparedness programmes.

low pressure zone.spoken: A low pressure zone usually means rain and strong winds are on the way.
low pressure zone.written: The formation of a low pressure zone over warm ocean waters can trigger the development of tropical cyclones of considerable intensity.

mudslide.spoken: Heavy rains caused a mudslide that buried several houses in the village below the hill.
mudslide.written: Mudslides, often precipitated by prolonged rainfall on deforested slopes, represent a growing hazard in regions affected by climate change.

normal cycle.spoken: Some people say global warming is just part of the Earth's normal cycle, but scientists disagree.
normal cycle.written: While natural variability contributes to the Earth's normal cycle of warming and cooling, the current rate of temperature increase far exceeds historical norms.

positive feedback.spoken: Melting ice is a positive feedback loop — less ice means less sunlight reflected, so it gets even warmer.
positive feedback.written: Positive feedback mechanisms, such as the ice-albedo effect, amplify initial warming and accelerate the rate of climate change.

rossby wave.spoken: Rossby waves in the atmosphere can cause weather patterns to get stuck in one place for weeks.
rossby wave.written: Rossby waves, which are large-scale meanders in high-altitude winds, significantly influence the trajectory and persistence of weather systems.

socioeconomic.spoken: Climate change hits poorer countries hardest, so it's a huge socioeconomic issue.
socioeconomic.written: The socioeconomic impacts of climate change are disproportionately borne by developing nations that lack the resources for effective adaptation.

tidal wave.spoken: The tidal wave was so powerful it swept away everything in its path along the coast.
tidal wave.written: A tidal wave generated by seismic activity can inundate coastal areas within minutes, leaving minimal time for evacuation.

trade wind.spoken: The trade winds basically drive the ocean currents in the tropics, which affects weather everywhere.
trade wind.written: Trade winds, which blow consistently from east to west in tropical latitudes, are integral to the circulation of both the atmosphere and the ocean.

upwelling.spoken: Upwelling brings cold, nutrient-rich water to the surface, which is great for marine life.
upwelling.written: Upwelling along continental margins supports some of the most productive fisheries on Earth by replenishing surface waters with essential nutrients.

warm pool.spoken: The Western Pacific warm pool is one of the warmest areas of ocean on the planet.
warm pool.written: The warm pool in the western Pacific Ocean plays a pivotal role in modulating global climate through its influence on atmospheric convection.

warm water.spoken: Warm water from the tropics gets carried north by ocean currents, which keeps Europe's climate mild.
warm water.written: The transport of warm water by ocean currents is a key mechanism through which thermal energy is redistributed across the planet.

beach.spoken: The beach has eroded so much over the past decade that it's barely half its original width now.
beach.written: Coastal beaches serve as natural buffers against storm surges, yet their ongoing erosion threatens both ecosystems and human settlements.

buffer.spoken: Wetlands act as a natural buffer against flooding, absorbing excess water during heavy rains.
buffer.written: The preservation of natural buffer zones along coastlines is essential for mitigating the impacts of rising sea levels and extreme weather events.

coastline.spoken: Our coastline is changing really fast because of rising sea levels and more frequent storms.
coastline.written: The management of a vulnerable coastline requires integrated strategies that balance economic development with environmental conservation.

dune.spoken: The sand dune system along the coast is actually really important for protecting inland areas from storms.
dune.written: Coastal dune systems function as natural barriers against marine erosion and storm surges, yet they are increasingly threatened by human activity.

ecological group.spoken: Each ecological group in the coastal ecosystem plays a specific role — remove one and the whole thing falls apart.
ecological group.written: The loss of any single ecological group within a coastal habitat can trigger cascading effects throughout the food web.

inland.spoken: As the coast erodes, people are being forced to move further inland to safer ground.
inland.written: The migration of populations from coastal areas to inland regions is expected to intensify as the effects of climate change become more pronounced.

oblivion.spoken: Some coastal towns have fallen into oblivion after repeated flooding drove everyone away.
oblivion.written: Without adequate intervention, many coastal heritage sites risk falling into oblivion as erosion and sea-level rise accelerate.

preservationist.spoken: The preservationist group's been fighting to stop developers from building on the coastal wetlands.
preservationist.written: Preservationist organisations play a crucial role in advocating for the protection of vulnerable coastal ecosystems from commercial exploitation.

retreat.spoken: The government's asking people in flood-prone areas to consider a managed retreat to higher ground.
retreat.written: A managed retreat from the most vulnerable coastal areas is increasingly being considered as a pragmatic adaptation strategy.

restoration.spoken: The restoration of the coral reef has been going really well — marine life is coming back.
restoration.written: The restoration of degraded coastal ecosystems, such as mangroves and coral reefs, is critical for enhancing resilience against climate-related hazards.

remedial.spoken: They've started taking remedial action to fix the damage done to the shoreline by years of neglect.
remedial.written: Remedial measures to address coastal erosion must be implemented promptly to prevent irreversible loss of land and habitat.

spoken: Heavy rainfall caused a lot of runoff, and the streets were completely flooded last week.
written: Excessive surface runoff, which is often exacerbated by urbanisation, can lead to severe soil erosion and water pollution.

spoken: The beach there is really sandy and soft — perfect for a family day out.
written: Sandy coastal regions are particularly vulnerable to erosion, which may be accelerated by rising sea levels.

spoken: We spent the whole afternoon just walking along the seashore collecting shells.
written: Seashore ecosystems are increasingly threatened by human activities, including coastal development and pollution.

spoken: They built a massive seawall to protect the town from the typhoon.
written: The construction of seawalls has been widely adopted as a coastal defence strategy against rising sea levels.

spoken: You can see layers of sediment at the bottom of the river when the water's clear.
written: Sediment accumulation in riverbeds, which is often caused by deforestation, can significantly alter aquatic habitats.

spoken: You can actually see alternating layers of lava flows in the cliff face — it looks amazing.
written: The geological record reveals alternating layers of lava flows, which provide valuable evidence of a volcano's eruption history.

spoken: After the eruption, tiny ash particles were floating in the air for days.
written: Fine ash particles released during volcanic eruptions can pose serious respiratory hazards to populations living downwind.

spoken: Hawaii is famous for basaltic lava flows that slowly creep down towards the ocean.
written: Basaltic lava, which is characterised by its low viscosity, tends to flow great distances before solidifying.

spoken: The blast from the explosion shattered windows in buildings two blocks away.
written: The blast generated by the eruption was so powerful that it was detected by monitoring stations thousands of kilometres away.

spoken: During the war, the city was hit by bomb after bomb — it was absolutely devastating.
written: The use of bombs in urban areas has been widely condemned by international organisations as a violation of humanitarian law.

spoken: From the top, you can look down into the bowl-shaped crater — it's massive.
written: A bowl-shaped crater is typically formed when a volcanic eruption empties the magma chamber beneath the summit.

spoken: You could see bubbles coming up from the mud pools — it was kind of eerie.
written: Gas bubbles that rise through volcanic lava provide scientists with crucial data about the chemical composition of magma.

spoken: The lake sits right inside a caldera formed by a massive eruption thousands of years ago.
written: A caldera is formed when a volcanic eruption is so massive that the ground above the emptied magma chamber collapses.

spoken: We hiked up this small cinder cone near the campsite — the view from the top was brilliant.
written: Cinder cones, which are the simplest type of volcano, are typically built by eruptions of small volumes of lava.

spoken: There's a strange circular depression in the field that some people think was made by a meteorite.
written: A circular depression may be formed by various geological processes, including volcanic activity, meteorite impacts, or subsidence.

spoken: Some of the symptoms turned out to be psychogenic illness rather than an actual physical condition.
written: Psychogenic illness, which originates in the mind rather than the body, can produce symptoms that closely mimic organic diseases.

spoken: We had our basement tested for radon because it can be really dangerous if it builds up.
written: Exposure to radon gas, which seeps into buildings from the ground, has been identified as the second leading cause of lung cancer.

spoken: The levels of respirable particulate matter in the city are way above safe limits right now.
written: Respirable particulate matter, when inhaled deep into the lungs, can cause irreversible damage to the respiratory system.

spoken: Since she got pregnant, she's developed a real sensitivity to odours — even coffee makes her feel sick.
written: Increased sensitivity to odours during pregnancy has been attributed to heightened hormonal activity affecting the olfactory system.

spoken: The sensory process behind how we taste food is actually way more complex than most people think.
written: The sensory process by which humans perceive their environment involves a series of complex neurological responses.

spoken: Babies rely on their sensory system to make sense of the world around them.
written: The sensory system enables organisms to detect and respond to environmental stimuli, which is essential for survival.

spoken: A lot of office workers complain about headaches, and it turns out their building has sick building syndrome.
written: Sick building syndrome, which causes a range of symptoms among building occupants, has been linked to poor indoor air quality.

spoken: The report confirmed that the office block was suffering from SBS due to inadequate ventilation.
written: SBS, or sick building syndrome, has become a significant concern for employers seeking to maintain a healthy workplace environment.

spoken: I got a really bad skin irritation after using that new detergent — my arms were covered in rashes.
written: Skin irritation caused by exposure to chemical irritants is one of the most commonly reported occupational health complaints.

spoken: Some household cleaners contain toxic chemical constituents that you really shouldn't breathe in.
written: The toxic chemical constituents found in industrial waste have been shown to cause severe damage to aquatic ecosystems.

spoken: The researchers identified a toxic compound in the water supply that was making people ill.
written: Toxic compounds emitted by industrial processes must be rigorously regulated to protect both public health and the environment.

spoken: According to the World Health Organisation, air pollution kills millions of people every year.
written: The World Health Organisation has established comprehensive guidelines for acceptable levels of various environmental pollutants.

spoken: When acidic juices from the stomach rise up, you get that horrible burning sensation in your chest.
written: The reflux of acidic juices from the stomach into the oesophagus can cause chronic inflammation and tissue damage.

spoken: I honestly couldn't survive summer here without air conditioning — it's way too humid.
written: Over-reliance on air conditioning systems may contribute to increased energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions.

spoken: If the soil's too acidic, you can add an alkali like lime to balance the pH.
written: The application of alkali to acidic soils has been a common agricultural practice for improving crop yields.

spoken: My little brother's asthmatic, so we always have to make sure there's an inhaler nearby.
written: Asthmatic patients are particularly susceptible to air quality deterioration, which can trigger severe respiratory episodes.

spoken: Bicarbonate helps neutralise the acid in your stomach — that's why antacids contain it.
written: Bicarbonate plays a crucial role in maintaining the body's acid-base balance, which is essential for normal cellular function.

spoken: The doctor explained that the problem was biochemical — basically, my body wasn't producing enough of a certain enzyme.
written: Biochemical processes within the human body are regulated by a complex network of enzymes and hormones.

spoken: Regular exercise and a healthy diet are key factors in cancer prevention, according to most doctors.
written: Cancer prevention strategies, which include lifestyle modifications and early screening programmes, have been shown to significantly reduce mortality rates.

spoken: Cigarette smoke contains loads of cancer-causing compounds — it's honestly shocking when you see the list.
written: The identification of cancer-causing compounds in consumer products has led to stricter regulatory standards in many countries.

spoken: Scientists are still studying the carcinogenicity of some chemicals used in food packaging.
written: The carcinogenicity of certain industrial chemicals has been well established through decades of epidemiological research.

spoken: My grandad was diagnosed with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease after smoking for forty years.
written: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease remains one of the leading causes of death worldwide, particularly in low-income countries.

spoken: People with chronic respiratory disease should really avoid going outside when pollution levels are high.
written: Chronic respiratory diseases, which affect hundreds of millions of people globally, impose a substantial economic burden on healthcare systems.

spoken: Eating too much fatty food increases your risk of coronary heart disease — that's what my doctor told me.
written: Coronary heart disease has been consistently linked to sedentary lifestyles and diets high in saturated fat.

spoken: The liver's main job is detoxification — it filters out all the harmful stuff in your blood.
written: The detoxification function of the liver is essential for removing metabolic waste products and harmful substances from the bloodstream.

spoken: Some people drink lemon water every morning claiming it helps the body detoxify naturally.
written: The human body has evolved sophisticated mechanisms to detoxify harmful substances, primarily through the liver and kidneys.

spoken: We all got really bad diarrhea after eating at that dodgy restaurant on holiday.
written: Diarrhea remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality among children in developing countries.

spoken: He was rushed to hospital with a bleeding duodenal ulcer — it was really frightening.
written: Duodenal ulcers, which are often caused by Helicobacter pylori infection, can be effectively treated with antibiotic therapy.

spoken: The duodenum is the first part of the small intestine — I learned that in biology class.
written: The duodenum plays a vital role in digestion by receiving partially digested food from the stomach and mixing it with bile.

spoken: Long-term smoking can lead to emphysema, which makes it really hard to breathe.
written: Emphysema, a progressive lung disease characterised by the destruction of alveolar walls, is predominantly caused by prolonged cigarette smoking.

spoken: Just breathe in slowly through your nose and then exhale through your mouth — it really helps you relax.
written: When humans exhale, carbon dioxide is released from the bloodstream into the lungs and expelled into the atmosphere.

spoken: Prolonged exposure to the sun without protection can seriously damage your skin.
written: Chronic exposure to environmental pollutants has been associated with an increased incidence of respiratory and cardiovascular diseases.

spoken: The chemicals in the pool gave me terrible eye irritation — I couldn't stop rubbing my eyes.
written: Eye irritation caused by airborne pollutants is a common complaint in urban areas with poor air quality.

spoken: Cleaning products can be real eye or respiratory irritants, so you should always open a window when using them.
written: Many household products contain eye or respiratory irritants that may pose health risks, particularly to vulnerable individuals.

spoken: Getting enough sleep is really important for keeping your immune system strong.
written: A well-functioning immune system is essential for protecting the body against a wide range of infectious diseases.

spoken: Patients on immunosuppressive drugs have to be extra careful about catching infections.
written: Immunosuppressive therapies, while necessary for preventing organ rejection, can leave patients highly susceptible to opportunistic infections.

spoken: The incidence of asthma has gone up a lot in cities — probably because of all the pollution.
written: The rising incidence of asthma in industrialised nations has prompted researchers to investigate the role of environmental factors.

spoken: Smoking doesn't just affect your health — it also drives up your incremental health insurance cost every year.
written: The incremental health insurance cost associated with chronic diseases places a significant financial burden on both individuals and public health systems.

spoken: The doctor said the inflammation in my knee should go down in a few days if I rest it properly.
written: Chronic inflammation has been identified as a contributing factor in the development of numerous diseases, including cancer and diabetes.

spoken: Inhalation of those fumes can cause headaches and dizziness, so wear a mask.
written: The inhalation of fine particulate matter has been directly linked to increased hospital admissions for respiratory and cardiovascular conditions.

spoken: Involuntary smoking is a real problem for kids whose parents smoke indoors.
written: Involuntary smoking, commonly known as secondhand smoke exposure, has been classified as a Group A carcinogen by health authorities.

spoken: Employers have a legal obligation to provide a safe working environment for their staff.
written: Companies have a legal obligation to ensure that their operations do not cause harm to the surrounding community or environment.

spoken: The legal status of e-cigarettes varies from country to country — some places have banned them entirely.
written: The legal status of alternative medicines differs significantly across jurisdictions, creating challenges for international regulation.

spoken: Cancer is a life-threatening disease, but early detection can really improve your chances.
written: Life-threatening diseases such as coronary heart disease and stroke remain the leading causes of mortality in developed nations.

spoken: The liver does an incredible job of filtering toxins from your blood — it's basically your body's detox centre.
written: The liver, which is the largest internal organ, performs over 500 essential functions including detoxification and protein synthesis.

spoken: As you get older, your metabolic rate slows down, which is why it's harder to lose weight.
written: Metabolic disorders, which disrupt normal biochemical processes in the body, can lead to a range of serious health complications.

spoken: When I tried to quit smoking, the nicotine withdrawal symptoms were absolutely awful — I was so irritable.
written: Nicotine withdrawal symptoms, which include anxiety, irritability, and difficulty concentrating, can persist for several weeks after cessation.

spoken: Passive smoking is just as dangerous as actually smoking — that's why they banned it in pubs.
written: Passive smoking has been conclusively shown to increase the risk of lung cancer and heart disease in non-smokers.

spoken: Stress and spicy food can make a peptic ulcer a lot worse, so you need to watch your diet.
written: Peptic ulcers, which develop in the lining of the stomach or duodenum, are most commonly caused by bacterial infection.

spoken: I've been coughing up phlegm all week — I think I need to see a doctor.
written: The excessive production of phlegm in the respiratory tract is a common symptom of chronic bronchitis and other respiratory conditions.

spoken: Air pollution is a major cause of respiratory disease, especially in big cities.
written: Respiratory diseases account for a significant proportion of global morbidity and mortality, particularly in low- and middle-income countries.

spoken: Kids are especially vulnerable to secondhand smoke, so parents really shouldn't smoke around them.
written: Secondhand smoke contains over 7,000 chemicals, many of which are known to cause cancer in humans.

spoken: The severity of symptoms varies from person to person — some people barely notice it while others are bedridden.
written: The severity of symptoms experienced by patients is influenced by a range of factors, including age, overall health, and environmental conditions.

spoken: Sidestream smoke — the smoke that comes off the end of a cigarette — is actually more toxic than what the smoker inhales.
written: Sidestream smoke, which is emitted between puffs, contains higher concentrations of carcinogens than mainstream smoke.

spoken: Since they introduced a smoke-free policy at work, the air quality in the office has improved loads.
written: The implementation of a smoke-free policy in public spaces has been shown to reduce hospital admissions for heart attacks.

spoken: My uncle finally quit after joining a smoking cessation programme at the local health centre.
written: Smoking cessation programmes, which combine behavioural counselling with pharmacological support, have proven to be the most effective approach to quitting.

spoken: You can use sodium bicarbonate — baking soda, basically — to get rid of nasty smells in the fridge.
written: Sodium bicarbonate is widely used in both medical and industrial applications due to its versatile chemical properties.

spoken: The air pollution was so bad yesterday that I had sore eyes and throat all evening.
written: Exposure to high levels of indoor pollutants can result in sore eyes and throat, headaches, and general fatigue.

spoken: Coughing and a runny nose are common symptoms of upper respiratory tract irritation.
written: Symptoms of upper respiratory tract irritation, such as nasal congestion and sore throat, are frequently reported by workers in poorly ventilated buildings.

spoken: A positive workplace culture can really make a difference to how happy and productive employees are.
written: Workplace health and safety regulations have been established to protect employees from occupational hazards and ensure safe working conditions.

spoken: I tried acupuncture for my back pain and honestly, it worked way better than I expected.
written: Acupuncture, which originated in ancient China, has been increasingly recognised as a viable complementary therapy for chronic pain management.

spoken: Caffeine is actually an alkaloid — it's a natural chemical found in coffee and tea plants.
written: Alkaloids, which are naturally occurring nitrogen-containing compounds, have been widely utilised in modern medicine for their pharmacological properties.

spoken: More and more people are turning to alternative and complementary medicine when conventional treatments don't work.
written: The growing popularity of alternative and complementary medicine has prompted researchers to evaluate the efficacy of these treatments through rigorous scientific studies.

spoken: ACM stands for alternative and complementary medicine — it covers things like herbal remedies and meditation.
written: ACM, or alternative and complementary medicine, encompasses a diverse range of therapeutic practices that fall outside conventional Western medicine.

spoken: Some people swear by alternative therapy like aromatherapy or homeopathy for stress relief.
written: Alternative therapies, while lacking robust clinical evidence for some conditions, continue to gain acceptance among patients and healthcare providers.

spoken: She uses aromatherapy with lavender oil to help her sleep — says it works wonders.
written: Aromatherapy, which involves the therapeutic use of essential oils, has been studied for its potential benefits in reducing anxiety and pain.

spoken: Ayurveda is an ancient Indian system of medicine — it's been around for thousands of years.
written: Ayurveda, a traditional Indian medical system, emphasises the balance of mind, body, and spirit for achieving optimal health.

spoken: Chinese medical therapy like herbal medicine and cupping has become really popular in the West recently.
written: Chinese medical therapy, which includes practices such as herbal medicine and acupuncture, has been used for over two millennia.

spoken: Many chronic and degenerative conditions like arthritis can be managed but not completely cured.
written: Chronic and degenerative conditions place an enormous strain on healthcare systems, requiring long-term management and sustained resource allocation.

spoken: The new drug is currently undergoing a clinical trial to see if it's safe and effective.
written: Clinical trials are essential for establishing the safety and efficacy of new pharmaceutical products before they can be approved for public use.

spoken: Complementary medicine is meant to be used alongside conventional treatment, not instead of it.
written: Complementary medicine, when used in conjunction with evidence-based treatments, may enhance patient outcomes and improve overall wellbeing.

spoken: Some patients prefer herbal remedies over conventional medical drugs because they think they have fewer side effects.
written: Conventional medical drugs undergo rigorous testing and approval processes, which distinguishes them from many alternative remedies.

spoken: When conventional methods fail, some people start looking into alternative approaches.
written: Conventional methods of treatment remain the cornerstone of modern healthcare, although their limitations have spurred interest in complementary approaches.

spoken: Getting an early diagnosis can make a huge difference when it comes to treating serious illnesses.
written: Accurate diagnosis is a prerequisite for effective treatment, yet many diseases remain difficult to identify in their early stages.

spoken: People have used herbs to treat all sorts of diseases and ailments for centuries.
written: A wide range of diseases and ailments may be alleviated through the integration of conventional and complementary medical approaches.

spoken: In traditional Chinese medicine, illness is seen as a kind of disharmony in the body.
written: Disharmony between the body's systems is considered a fundamental cause of disease in many traditional medical philosophies.

spoken: We need to do more to protect endangered species before it's too late.
written: The protection of endangered species requires coordinated international efforts and the enforcement of strict anti-poaching legislation.

spoken: Some plants produce glycosides that can be toxic if you eat them in large amounts.
written: Glycosides, which are naturally occurring compounds in many plants, have been utilised in the development of cardiac medications.

spoken: Hepatitis is a serious liver infection — you can get vaccinated against some types.
written: Hepatitis, which causes inflammation of the liver, can lead to chronic disease, cirrhosis, and liver cancer if left untreated.

spoken: I've started adding fresh herb to my cooking, and honestly, it makes such a difference to the flavour.
written: It has been widely acknowledged that the incorporation of herb into one's diet can significantly enhance nutritional intake and overall well-being.

spoken: My grandma's really into herbal remedies — she's always brewing some kind of tea for every little ailment.
written: The growing popularity of herbal supplements has prompted researchers to investigate their efficacy and potential side effects in clinical settings.

spoken: A lot of people I know prefer herbal medicine over conventional drugs because they think it's got fewer side effects.
written: It is argued that herbal medicine should be integrated into mainstream healthcare systems to provide patients with a broader range of treatment options.

spoken: She went to see a herbal practitioner who recommended a whole bunch of natural treatments for her migraines.
written: Consulting a qualified herbal practitioner is often recommended for individuals seeking alternative approaches to managing chronic conditions.

spoken: My neighbour swears by this herbalist she visits — she reckons he's sorted out her digestion problems completely.
written: The role of the herbalist in community healthcare has been documented across numerous cultures throughout human history.

spoken: I reckon holistic medicine is becoming way more popular because people want to treat the whole person, not just the symptoms.
written: Holistic medicine, which addresses the physical, emotional, and spiritual dimensions of health, has gained considerable traction in recent decades.

spoken: Some of my friends are really into homeopathy, though I'm not entirely convinced it actually works, to be honest.
written: Despite ongoing debate within the scientific community, homeopathy continues to attract a significant number of practitioners and patients worldwide.

spoken: After a really long week at work, I love getting a massage to help me unwind and de-stress.
written: Therapeutic massage has been shown to reduce cortisol levels and promote relaxation, thereby contributing to improved mental health outcomes.

spoken: The medicine industry is massive, isn't it? Companies spend billions on research and development every single year.
written: The global medicine industry has been criticised for prioritising profit over accessibility, particularly in developing nations.

spoken: Have you ever tried moxibustion? It's this traditional therapy where they burn dried herbs near your skin — sounds a bit scary but it's actually quite soothing.
written: Moxibustion, a key component of traditional Chinese medicine, has been practised for centuries and is believed to stimulate the flow of vital energy.

spoken: I've been looking into natural therapy options like aromatherapy and acupuncture — I'd rather avoid medication if I can.
written: Natural therapy encompasses a wide range of treatments that utilise the body's inherent healing mechanisms rather than relying on pharmaceutical interventions.

spoken: My sister's training to be a naturopathy practitioner — she's really passionate about treating people with natural methods.
written: Naturopathy is founded on the principle that the body possesses an intrinsic ability to heal itself when provided with the appropriate conditions.

spoken: When you're really ill, orthodox drug treatments are usually the most reliable option, wouldn't you say?
written: Orthodox drug therapies have undergone rigorous clinical trials to establish their safety and efficacy before being approved for public use.

spoken: I think orthodox medical treatment should always be the first port of call, but it's good to know about other options as well.
written: Orthodox medical treatment, although highly effective for many conditions, may not adequately address the psychological and emotional needs of patients.

spoken: What people really want is a personalised and individual form of treatment that takes into account their specific circumstances.
written: A personalised and individual form of treatment is increasingly regarded as essential for achieving optimal patient outcomes in contemporary healthcare.

spoken: The pharmaceutic industry is always coming up with new drugs — it's hard to keep track of all the developments.
written: The pharmaceutic sector has been instrumental in extending life expectancy through the development of innovative medications and vaccines.

spoken: Good post-operative care is absolutely vital — the recovery period can make or break the success of the surgery.
written: Effective post-operative care has been identified as a critical factor in reducing hospital readmission rates and improving surgical outcomes.

spoken: It's really important to find a practitioner you trust — I went through a few before I found the right one.
written: The competence of a healthcare practitioner directly influences the quality of care received by patients and their subsequent recovery.

spoken: I tried reflexology last month where they massage specific points on your feet — it was surprisingly relaxing actually.
written: Reflexology, which involves the application of pressure to specific points on the feet, is believed to promote healing in corresponding areas of the body.

spoken: The whole point of rehabilitation is to restore normal function so you can get back to your everyday life.
written: The primary objective of any medical intervention should be to restore normal function and enable patients to resume their daily activities.

spoken: Different therapists have different therapeutic approaches, so it's worth shopping around to find what works best for you.
written: An effective therapeutic approach should be tailored to the individual needs of the patient, taking into account both physical and psychological factors.

spoken: Seeing a therapist was honestly one of the best decisions I've ever made — it really helped me manage my anxiety.
written: A skilled therapist can equip individuals with the coping strategies necessary to navigate life's challenges and maintain psychological well-being.

spoken: Traditional Chinese medicine has been around for thousands of years — my parents swear by acupuncture and herbal remedies.
written: Traditional Chinese medicine, with its holistic approach to diagnosis and treatment, has attracted increasing interest from the Western medical community.

spoken: The traditional philosophy of treatment in many Asian cultures focuses on balancing the body's energy rather than just fighting symptoms.
written: The traditional philosophy of treatment emphasises harmony between the individual and their environment, a concept that remains relevant in modern healthcare.

spoken: Western drug therapy has come a long way, but some people still prefer natural alternatives for treating minor conditions.
written: While Western drug therapy has yielded remarkable results in combating infectious diseases, its limitations in treating chronic conditions have prompted the exploration of alternative modalities.

spoken: Western herbal medicine uses plants that are commonly found in Europe and North America, which is quite different from Eastern traditions.
written: Western herbal medicine draws upon a rich tradition of botanical knowledge that has been refined through centuries of empirical observation and practice.

spoken: The yin yang principle is all about balance — when one side's off, your health suffers, according to Chinese philosophy.
written: The yin yang principle, which posits that health is achieved through the equilibrium of opposing forces, forms the philosophical foundation of traditional Chinese medicine.

spoken: She's got such a lovely complexion — I think it's because she drinks loads of water and eats really well.
written: Research has indicated that dietary habits and hydration levels have a direct impact on an individual's complexion and skin health.

spoken: I always get more freckle in the summer — they pop up all over my nose and cheeks whenever I'm in the sun.
written: The appearance of freckle is primarily determined by genetic factors and is exacerbated by prolonged exposure to ultraviolet radiation.

spoken: The incidence rate of skin cancer has gone up massively over the past few decades, which is really alarming.
written: The incidence rate of melanoma has risen steadily in recent years, a trend that has been attributed to increased ultraviolet exposure and inadequate sun protection.

spoken: My uncle was diagnosed with melanoma last year, so now I'm extra careful about wearing sunscreen whenever I go out.
written: Melanoma, one of the most aggressive forms of skin cancer, is responsible for a disproportionate number of skin cancer-related deaths worldwide.

spoken: I've got this naevus on my arm that the doctor said I should keep an eye on in case it changes shape.
written: A naevus, commonly referred to as a mole, should be monitored regularly for any changes in size, colour, or texture that may indicate malignancy.

spoken: The occurrence of extreme weather events seems to be increasing every year — it's quite worrying, isn't it?
written: The increasing occurrence of natural disasters has been directly linked to climate change and rising global temperatures.

spoken: It's good news that the ozone layer is slowly recovering, but we still need to be careful about harmful chemicals.
written: The depletion of the ozone layer remains a significant environmental concern, as it allows greater quantities of harmful ultraviolet radiation to reach the Earth's surface.

spoken: Workers at nuclear plants have to be really careful about radiation exposure — there are strict limits in place.
written: Prolonged radiation exposure has been associated with an elevated risk of developing various forms of cancer and other serious health conditions.

spoken: I got really bad sunburn on holiday last year — my shoulders were peeling for days, it was awful.
written: Severe sunburn during childhood has been identified as a significant risk factor for the development of skin cancer in later life.

spoken: I never go to the beach without putting on sunscreen — I learned my lesson after getting badly burned a few years ago.
written: The regular application of sunscreen with a high SPF has been proven to significantly reduce the risk of ultraviolet-induced skin damage.

spoken: Everyone wants a nice suntan in the summer, but you've got to be careful not to overdo it and damage your skin.
written: Although a suntan is often perceived as a sign of health, it actually represents the skin's defensive response to ultraviolet-induced DNA damage.

spoken: Some people have a higher susceptibility to sun damage because of their skin type — fair-skinned people need to be extra careful.
written: Individual susceptibility to skin cancer is influenced by a combination of genetic predisposition, environmental factors, and behavioural patterns.

spoken: I tan really easily, which sounds great but actually means I'm more at risk of skin damage from the sun.
written: The ability to tan varies significantly among individuals and is primarily determined by the concentration of melanin in the skin.

spoken: Indoor tanning is actually really dangerous — using sunbeds can massively increase your chances of getting skin cancer.
written: Artificial tanning through the use of sunbeds has been classified as a carcinogen by the World Health Organisation, yet it remains alarmingly popular among young adults.

spoken: You can't actually see ultraviolet light, but it's the main thing that causes sunburn and long-term skin damage.
written: Ultraviolet radiation, which is invisible to the human eye, is the primary environmental factor responsible for the majority of skin cancers.
